activities_projects.title: Verifying Mass Conservation
activities_projects.objective: To observe that mass remains unchanged during a chemical reaction[cite: 1439, 1440].
steps: Prepare 5% solutions of barium chloride and sodium sulphate[cite: 1448].
Place sodium sulphate solution in a conical flask and barium chloride in a small ignition tube[cite: 1449].
Hang the tube in the flask, seal with a cork, and weigh the entire setup[cite: 1450, 1451, 1460].
Tilt the flask to mix the solutions, observe the reaction, and weigh it again[cite: 1461, 1462].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Confirms the Law of Conservation of Mass: mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction[cite: 1466].
activities_projects.evaluation: Compare the mass of the flask before and after mixing the chemicals (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Visualizing Atomic Size
activities_projects.objective: To understand the scale of atoms compared to everyday objects[cite: 1516, 1517].
steps: Observe the thickness of a single sheet of paper[cite: 1518].
Discuss that millions of atoms stacked would barely equal this thickness[cite: 1518].
Compare the relative sizes of an atom of hydrogen ($10^{-10}$ m) to a grain of sand ($10^{-4}$ m)[cite: 1519].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that atoms are the fundamental building blocks of all matter despite being invisible to the naked eye[cite: 1515, 1521].
activities_projects.evaluation: List the relative sizes of a water molecule and an ant in meters (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Symbol Matching Game
activities_projects.objective: To learn modern-day chemical symbols for various elements[cite: 1526, 1552].
steps: Review the rules for symbols: first letter is capital, second is small (e.g., Al for Aluminium)[cite: 1554, 1558].
Match English-derived symbols like Hydrogen (H) and Carbon (C)[cite: 1556, 1574].
Match Latin-derived symbols like Iron (Fe) and Sodium (Na)[cite: 1562, 1574].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Correctly identifies unique chemical symbols for common elements[cite: 1563].
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the symbols for Calcium, Copper, and Potassium (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Atomic Mass Scale Model
activities_projects.objective: To understand relative atomic mass using a watermelon analogy[cite: 1569, 1590].
steps: Assume a watermelon represents 12 units of mass[cite: 1591].
Cut it into 12 equal pieces to represent 1 unit each (relative mass unit)[cite: 1592].
Weigh other fruits against these 'units' to find their relative weight[cite: 1593].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that atomic mass is a relative value compared to $1/12^{th}$ the mass of a carbon-12 atom[cite: 1588, 1589].
activities_projects.evaluation: State the atomic mass of Oxygen and Magnesium in 'u' (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Building Molecule Models
activities_projects.objective: To differentiate between molecules of elements and compounds[cite: 1616, 1650].
steps: Make two identical clay balls and join them to represent Oxygen ($O_2$)[cite: 1620].
Make three identical balls to represent Ozone ($O_3$)[cite: 1621].
Join different colored balls in a 2:1 ratio to represent Water ($H_2O$)[cite: 1654].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that molecules can be made of the same or different types of atoms[cite: 1612].
activities_projects.evaluation: Identify the atomicity of Argon, Helium, and Phosphorus (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Ion Identification Activity
activities_projects.objective: To distinguish between positively and negatively charged species[cite: 1655, 1656].
steps: Learn that a positively charged ion is a 'cation' and a negative one is an 'anion'[cite: 1658].
Identify common ions like Sodium ($Na^+$) and Chloride ($Cl^-$)[cite: 1659].
List polyatomic ions like Ammonium ($NH_4^+$) and Hydroxide ($OH^-$)[cite: 1662, 1679].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that ionic compounds consist of balanced charged particles[cite: 1712].
activities_projects.evaluation: Classify $Mg^{2+}$ and $SO_4^{2-}$ as cation, anion, or polyatomic ion (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Criss-Cross Formula Game
activities_projects.objective: To write chemical formulae using valency[cite: 1666, 1671].
steps: Write the symbols of constituent elements side-by-side[cite: 1693].
Write their valencies/charges below the symbols[cite: 1693].
Crossover the valencies to determine the subscripts (e.g., $MgCl_2$)[cite: 1694, 1710].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies valency rules to determine the chemical formula of simple compounds[cite: 1845].
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the chemical formula for Sodium Oxide and Aluminium Chloride (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Calculating Molecular Mass
activities_projects.objective: To find the relative mass of a molecule[cite: 1787, 1789].
steps: Identify the atoms in a molecule (e.g., Water has 2H and 1O)[cite: 1809].
Find the atomic masses (H=1, O=16) from Table 3.2[cite: 1600, 1807].
Sum the masses: $(2 	imes 1) + (1 	imes 16) = 18$ u[cite: 1809, 1810].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Calculates the molecular mass of various substances in atomic mass units[cite: 1790].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the molecular mass of Carbon Dioxide ($CO_2$) (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: The Octopus Valency Model
activities_projects.objective: To visualize valency as 'arms' of an atom[cite: 1670, 1672].
steps: Assign 8 arms to the 'Octopus' (O) and 2 arms to each 'Human' (H)[cite: 1673].
Try to lock all arms so everyone is held securely[cite: 1674].
Observe that 1 Octopus can hold 4 Humans, forming $OH_4$[cite: 1675, 1676].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that valency represents the combining power of an element[cite: 1670].
activities_projects.evaluation: If an atom has valency 3, how many Hydrogen atoms (valency 1) can it hold? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Naming Ionic Compounds
activities_projects.objective: To learn the rules for naming metal and non-metal combinations[cite: 1684].
materials: Flashcards of compounds like CaO, NaCl, FeS
steps: Observe that the metal name is always written first (e.g., Calcium in CaO)[cite: 1686].
Note that non-metals are written on the right (e.g., Chlorine in NaCl)[cite: 1687].
Identify polyatomic ions within formulas like $NaOH$ or $Mg(OH)_2$[cite: 1689].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Correctly names and writes symbols for ionic compounds[cite: 1846].
activities_projects.evaluation: Name the compound represented by the formula $CaCl_2$ (10 marks).
